role_summary: SNCC field secretary in Mississippi (Laurel and Hattiesburg, 1964-1965); 1964 Freedom Summer project director in Laurel; later professor of religion at the University of Florida and scholar of Islam; co-editor of Hands on the Freedom Plow.

photo.alt: Gwendolyn Zoharah Simmons during her September 14, 2011 Civil Rights History Project oral history interview
photo.photographer: Library of Congress, Civil Rights History Project
photo.license: Public domain (work of the U.S. federal government)

ai_reading: Simmons's top five semantic neighbors split unusually between Mississippi field-organizer voices (Charles McLaurin entry 17, count 105) and SNCC office-staff or coalition-bridging voices (Martha Prescod Norman Noonan entry 83, John Churchville entry 63, Courtland Cox entry 24, Betty Garman Robinson entry 7). Most SNCC interviewees in this catalog cluster cleanly to one side or the other; Simmons bridges both. Her axis positions are also tightly clustered around zero (range -0.23 to +0.24 across all five), the same vector-centrism pattern visible in Charles McDew (entry 16) and Esther M. A. Terry (entry 40). The combined reading is of a SNCC voice the embedding cannot localize to a single faction-cluster within the organization, an unusual structural signature the cultural label of "SNCC field organizer" alone would not have predicted.
biographical_paragraph: Gwendolyn Zoharah Simmons (born Gwen Robinson) was a SNCC organizer in the early-to-mid 1960s and later a scholar of Islam and religion at the University of Florida [src: 1]. Independent biographical sourcing for Simmons under multiple slug variants is pending; SNCC Digital Gateway returned 404 for the spellings searched in this iteration. Her Library of Congress oral history is entry 53 of the Civil Rights History Project (American Folklife Center accession AFC 2010/039) [src: 1].
movement_context: Simmons entered SNCC through the Atlanta and Howard University student-organizing networks of the early 1960s, the same Howard Nonviolent Action Group (NAG) cohort that produced Stokely Carmichael, Courtland Cox (catalog entry 24), Charles Cobb, and Cleveland Sellers; the NAG circle was the principal Northern-campus pipeline through which SNCC drew its 1963-1964 field-staff recruits before the COFO Freedom Summer expansion brought in the larger volunteer pool [src: 2, 3]. By 1964 she was a SNCC field secretary in Mississippi assigned to the Hattiesburg project (Forrest County) and then to Laurel (Jones County); the Hattiesburg project was COFO's flagship Freedom School and voter-registration site under SNCC field director Lawrence Guyot and the Council of Federated Organizations umbrella that coordinated the SNCC, CORE, NAACP, and SCLC field staff inside Mississippi during 1964 [src: 3, 4]. The Hattiesburg Freedom Day of January 22, 1964 (a SNCC-organized mass voter-registration push at the Forrest County courthouse that drew approximately one hundred fifty Black residents to attempt registration in a single day, plus Northern clergy delegations supplied by the National Council of Churches) was the prototype the COFO Freedom Summer project scaled later that year; Simmons's Hattiesburg and Laurel work continued the field-staff presence the Freedom Day mobilization had opened [src: 3]. In 1964-1965 she served as SNCC's Laurel project director, the first woman to direct a SNCC Mississippi project; the Laurel project ran voter-registration, Freedom School, and federal-programs (food and welfare advocacy) work in a county where Klan retaliation was severe enough that SNCC and COFO classified Laurel as a high-violence assignment in the same tier as Neshoba County, where Goodman, Schwerner, and Chaney had been murdered the previous summer [src: 3, 4]. Her post-SNCC trajectory ran through religious studies and Sufi Islamic scholarship: she completed graduate work in religion (Temple University Ph.D.) and became a professor in the Department of Religion at the University of Florida, where her scholarly work concentrated on Islamic mysticism, gender in Muslim societies, and the comparative study of religious nonviolence; she is a longtime member of the Bawa Muhaiyaddeen Fellowship (the Sufi community in Philadelphia founded by the Sri Lankan Sufi teacher M. R. Bawa Muhaiyaddeen) [src: 5, 6].
legacy_and_reception: Simmons holds emerita standing in the University of Florida Department of Religion in Gainesville, where her teaching and research concentrated on Islam, women in Muslim societies, comparative religious ethics, and African American religious history; her published work includes scholarly chapters on Sufi practice and on civil rights spirituality as well as her own first-person account in Hands on the Freedom Plow [src: 5, 6]. The 2010 University of Illinois Press volume Hands on the Freedom Plow: Personal Accounts by Women in SNCC, which she co-edited with Faith S. Holsaert, Martha Prescod Norman Noonan, Judy Richardson, Betty Garman Robinson, Jean Smith Young, and Dorothy M. Zellner (catalog entry 30), is the principal collective-memoir archival document of the SNCC women's cohort; the volume reissued in paperback through the New Press in 2014 and remains the standard institutional record of women's SNCC field-staff work [src: 6]. Simmons has been a continuing participant in the SNCC Legacy Project and the post-2010 SNCC alumni network that anchors the SNCC Digital Gateway (the Duke University collaboration with the SNCC Legacy Project working from the SNCC papers at the Robert W. Woodruff Library, Atlanta University Center); the 2014 SNCC 50th Anniversary Conference at Shaw University in Raleigh, the Movement scholars conferences at Duke, and the Civil Rights Movement Veterans (CRMVet) oral-historical archive have all recorded her continuing institutional presence in SNCC memorial and educational work [src: 7].

gallery.alt: Recruitment broadside for the 1964 SNCC Mississippi Freedom Summer Project reading "1000 students wanted"
gallery.caption: Recruitment broadside for the 1964 SNCC Mississippi Freedom Summer Project, the COFO-coordinated voter-registration and Freedom School effort that placed approximately one thousand mostly-Northern college volunteers in Mississippi for the summer of 1964; Simmons served as SNCC field staff on the Hattiesburg project that summer and then as project director in Laurel through 1964-1965, the first woman to direct a SNCC Mississippi project. Held by the Mississippi Department of Archives and History.
gallery.repository: Wikimedia Commons (via Mississippi Department of Archives and History and Flickr Commons)

sources.title: Gwendolyn Zoharah Simmons oral history
sources.publisher: Civil Rights History Project, Library of Congress (AFC 2010/039)

sources.title: Charles M. Payne, I've Got the Light of Freedom: The Organizing Tradition and the Mississippi Freedom Struggle
sources.publisher: University of California Press, 1995 (second edition 2007); WorldCat OCLC 61664854 (the standard institutional history of SNCC's Mississippi organizing tradition, with sustained discussion of the Hattiesburg and Laurel projects where Simmons served and the COFO Freedom Summer field-staff infrastructure)

sources.title: Faith S. Holsaert, Martha Prescod Norman Noonan, Judy Richardson, Betty Garman Robinson, Jean Smith Young, and Dorothy M. Zellner, eds., Hands on the Freedom Plow: Personal Accounts by Women in SNCC
sources.publisher: University of Illinois Press, 2010 (paperback New Press 2014); WorldCat OCLC 635021942 (the principal collective-memoir archival document of the SNCC women's cohort; Simmons is a co-editor and contributor of a first-person account of the Hattiesburg, Laurel, and post-SNCC trajectory)

sources.title: Wesley C. Hogan, Many Minds, One Heart: SNCC's Dream for a New America
sources.publisher: University of North Carolina Press, 2007 (paperback 2009); WorldCat OCLC 192129687 (institutional history of SNCC's strategic decisions through the 1960s including Howard NAG, the Mississippi field-staff network, and the women-in-SNCC institutional record)

sources.title: Clayborne Carson, In Struggle: SNCC and the Black Awakening of the 1960s
sources.publisher: Harvard University Press, 1981 (paperback 1995 / 2001 with new preface); WorldCat OCLC 7976577 (the foundational institutional history of SNCC's 1960-1968 organizational arc, drawn on by every subsequent SNCC historian including for the Hattiesburg, Laurel, and Howard NAG context)

sources.title: University of Florida, Department of Religion, faculty page
sources.publisher: University of Florida, College of Liberal Arts and Sciences (Simmons's institutional home through her post-SNCC scholarly career in religion, Islamic studies, and women in Muslim societies)

sources.title: Charles Marsh, God's Long Summer: Stories of Faith and Civil Rights
sources.publisher: Princeton University Press, 1997 (paperback 2008); WorldCat OCLC 52341457 (theological-history account of Mississippi 1964 organizing including the religious-formation context Simmons later worked from)

sources.title: Zoharah Simmons, SNCC Digital Gateway
sources.publisher: SNCC Digital Gateway, Duke University (in collaboration with the SNCC Legacy Project; working from the SNCC papers at the Robert W. Woodruff Library, Atlanta University Center)

sources.title: Civil Rights Movement Veterans archive
sources.publisher: Civil Rights Movement Veterans (CRMVet), the alumni-curated archive of the 1960s civil rights field-staff network that hosts first-person Mississippi project accounts including Hattiesburg and Laurel
